title: linehash v1 implementation program
description:
Purpose:
Create the complete implementation graph for linehash v1 as a self-contained delivery program, not just a loose task list. This epic exists to preserve the overall intent of the project: safe, predictable, low-friction, hash-anchored editing for agent workflows.

Background / why this matters:
- The project is intentionally conservative: reject stale, ambiguous, or unsafe edits rather than guessing.
- The implementation order matters because many later commands build directly on a few foundational primitives: hashing, document loading, anchor parsing/resolution, output formatting, and atomic writeback.
- The bead graph should make the critical path obvious, while still exposing parallelizable work once the core stabilizes.

Success definition:
- All v1 acceptance criteria in PLAN.md are represented by concrete, actionable issues.
- Dependencies reflect architectural truth rather than convenience.
- Future sessions can understand not only what to do, but why the sequence exists and what invariants must be preserved.

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: This top-level epic was created from PLAN.md as a self-contained program map. The structure intentionally mirrors the architecture: spec -> bootstrap -> foundation -> core commands -> transaction safety -> power features / workflow extensions -> hardening / release. Use this as the canonical work graph for implementation sequencing.

title: Freeze spec invariants and implementation contracts
description:
Purpose:
Freeze and restate the non-negotiable invariants from the plan in implementation-ready form so no future work accidentally weakens the tool's safety model.

Key invariants to preserve:
- Hash exact raw line content excluding only newline terminators.
- Preserve newline style and trailing newline status exactly.
- UTF-8 only in v1.
- Canonical anchor display/input model is based on 2-char hashes with optional line qualification.
- Anchor resolution is safety-first: no silent retargeting.
- Mutation commands accept only single logical lines in v1.
- Whole-file load/transform/writeback is acceptable and preferred for simplicity/auditability.

Why this is first:
These choices determine correctness. Everything downstream assumes them.

Done means:
- The codebase structure and all implementation tickets align with the frozen spec.
- Any contradiction discovered during implementation is captured and reconciled explicitly rather than patched over locally.

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Revision note: added explicit plan-space tasks for spec audit, UX contract review, and test mapping. This makes it cheaper to improve the product before implementation hardens assumptions into code.

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Second refinement: added an explicit command-surface review bead to challenge whether the planned v1 is the best first release for users, rather than simply the most feature-complete one.

title: Audit PLAN.md for internal consistency and hidden implementation assumptions
description:
Purpose:
Perform a dedicated spec-audit pass over PLAN.md before implementation begins.

Why this bead was added:
The original graph jumped from "freeze spec" straight into implementation preparation. That risks discovering contradictions too late. A separate audit bead creates an explicit checkpoint for detecting internal inconsistencies, unclear contracts, or accidental overreach while changes are still cheap.

Specific review targets:
- Internal consistency across the scope list, file structure, data model, CLI contract, phases, tests, and acceptance criteria.
- Places where the plan implicitly promises behavior that is not fully represented in the proposed module APIs.
- Places where later command descriptions depend on hidden helper abstractions that are not yet modeled as tasks.

Done means:
- Any contradictions or under-specified behaviors are captured and resolved in beads/comments before implementation starts.

title: Review and lock user-facing CLI/JSON/error contract before implementation
description:
Purpose:
Review the CLI/output contract from the user's perspective and lock down behavior that most affects usability, trust, and agent integration.

Why this bead was added:
The original graph focused mostly on architecture. This task makes user value explicit in plan space: stable JSON schema, helpful error/hint wording, discoverability of commands, output predictability, and consistency across pretty vs JSON paths.

Focus areas:
- Which outputs should be considered stable interfaces for agents.
- Whether any command defaults should be reconsidered for safer or lower-friction use.
- Whether any names, flags, or error wording should change before implementation.
- Whether any command should gain/lose a dependency on output machinery.

Done means:
- A documented UX contract exists in bead comments/notes sufficient to guide implementation decisions without ad hoc tweaks later.

title: Map acceptance criteria and invariants to planned test coverage
description:
Purpose:
Create a direct traceability map from acceptance criteria and module contracts to tests.

Why this bead was added:
The original graph had good testing intent but not an explicit planning artifact for coverage mapping. This bead prevents orphaned promises by making the test strategy concrete before implementation starts.

Done means:
- Each major acceptance criterion and module invariant is linked to at least one planned unit/integration/snapshot test category.
- Gaps are identified before code creates inertia.

title: Review and tune v1 command surface for maximum user value
description:
Purpose:
Review whether any v1 commands should be deferred, merged, or reshaped to maximize user value per implementation cost before coding begins.

Why this bead was added:
The PLAN is ambitious for a v1. This task explicitly asks whether the current command surface is the best first release for users, or whether a smaller/more coherent subset would produce a better first shipped product.

Questions to answer:
- Are all listed v1 commands truly v1-worthy?
- Which commands produce the highest user value earliest?
- Are any commands better framed as post-v1 once the core interaction model is proven?

Done means:
- The intended v1 surface is explicitly justified in bead notes/comments, or revised before implementation begins.

title: Bootstrap Rust workspace and CLI skeleton
description:
Purpose:
Bootstrap the Rust workspace exactly as described in the plan: workspace root only for config/docs, source code in crates/core, and a thin bin entry point.

Why:
A clean workspace layout keeps implementation auditable and makes future crate extraction possible without churn.

Done means:
- Workspace and crate manifests are in place.
- Entry point exists and subcommands parse.
- The repository can run formatting/tests even before full functionality exists.

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Revision note: added a command-framework task so each command does not independently reinvent dispatch, JSON routing, and stdout/stderr conventions.

title: Create workspace manifests and clap CLI skeleton
description:
Create the clap-based CLI skeleton, workspace manifests, commands module layout, and thin main entry point.

Why:
This establishes the shape of the codebase so later work lands in predictable files rather than being refactored repeatedly.

Done means:
- Root Cargo.toml and crates/core/Cargo.toml match the planned structure closely.
- main.rs is thin.
- cli.rs defines the subcommand surface.
- commands/mod.rs exists with placeholders for planned commands.

title: Set up test harness, helpers, and fixture corpus
description:
Set up test infrastructure and core fixtures.

Background:
The plan relies heavily on integration and snapshot tests. Early fixture/helper setup prevents every later command task from re-solving the same scaffolding problems.

Scope:
- tests/ layout
- representative fixtures for LF, CRLF, no trailing newline, empty, mixed newline, invalid UTF-8/binary
- reusable helpers for temp files and command assertions

Done means:
- Foundational command tasks can add focused tests without duplicating fixture setup.

title: Establish shared command execution and output-routing pattern
description:
Purpose:
Create a consistent command execution framework tying clap inputs to domain modules, stdout/stderr behavior, JSON mode routing, and exit codes.

Why this bead was added:
The original graph had CLI skeleton and then command tasks, but no explicit bead for the cross-cutting command glue. That makes it easy for each command to evolve slightly different conventions. A dedicated framework task improves consistency for users and reduces implementation drift.

Scope:
- Common command dispatch conventions
- Shared JSON flag handling pattern
- Error-to-exit-code handling via thin main
- Standardized writer usage for testability

Done means:
- New commands can follow one established pattern instead of re-deciding command plumbing each time.

title: Build core foundation modules
description:
Purpose:
Build the foundational runtime model: hashing, document loading, anchor parsing/resolution, output formatting, and atomic writeback.

Why:
These modules are the substrate for every command. If they are wrong, every higher-level feature is wrong.

Sequencing rationale:
- Hashing must exist before lines can be indexed.
- Document loading must exist before anchors can resolve.
- Anchor resolution must exist before command safety can be guaranteed.
- Output formatting must exist to make early commands testable and agent-usable.
- Atomic writeback must exist before any mutation command ships.

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Foundation work is intentionally front-loaded because later commands are thin orchestrators over these modules. Any shortcut here tends to reappear later as either data corruption risk or output instability.

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Revision note: added a shared mutation-engine task. This reduces future duplication across edit/insert/delete/patch/swap/move/indent/explode-implode paths and should improve behavioral consistency for users.

title: Implement hash.rs primitives and tests
description:
Implement hash.rs exactly to spec.

Background:
The short hash is the anchor primitive, so determinism and formatting correctness matter more than cleverness. The function must hash exact raw line content and expose a 2-char lowercase hex representation derived from the low byte of xxh32(seed=0).

Key considerations:
- No trimming.
- Empty string is a normal line.
- Keep full-hash vs short-hash logic isolated to make future hash-length expansion straightforward.

Done means:
- Public API matches the plan.
- Unit tests cover determinism, formatting, whitespace sensitivity, and collision helper behavior.

title: Implement document.rs loading, rendering, and indexing
description:
Implement document.rs and the in-memory Document model.

Background:
Document loading is where file bytes become the authoritative internal model used by every command. This is also where newline style, trailing newline, binary detection, UTF-8 validation, and file metadata are captured.

Key considerations:
- Preserve exact formatting semantics on render.
- Reject mixed newlines and invalid UTF-8 explicitly.
- Empty-file behavior must match the plan exactly.
- Build line records with both full and short hashes.

Done means:
- load/from_str/build_index/render/len/is_empty exist and obey the specified edge cases.
- Unit tests cover LF, CRLF, empty, no trailing newline, whitespace-only lines, invalid UTF-8, binary files, index collisions, and metadata capture.

title: Define LinehashError and recovery-oriented error contract
description:
Create the central error model in error.rs.

Background:
The plan is unusually explicit about user-facing recovery hints. That means the error model is not incidental plumbing; it is part of the product contract.

Key considerations:
- Cover the full set of planned error conditions, even if some are only used later.
- Preserve actionable wording and recovery hints.
- Keep variants structured enough for tests and command-specific formatting.

Done means:
- The core LinehashError enum exists and can support foundation and later modules without ad hoc string errors.

title: Implement anchor parsing and safety-first resolution
description:
Implement anchor.rs parsing and resolution.

Background:
This module enforces the project's safety model. It must reject ambiguity and staleness conservatively rather than guessing user intent.

Key considerations:
- Normalize uppercase input but keep canonical lowercase display.
- Qualified anchors must validate the current line's hash, not search for a convenient replacement.
- Range anchors are inclusive and must enforce start <= end.
- Bulk resolution should collect failures for verify/patch workflows.

Done means:
- parse_anchor, parse_range, resolve, resolve_range, and resolve_all exist and pass the plan's required unit tests.

title: Implement output formatting for pretty and JSON modes
description:
Implement output.rs for pretty and JSON formatting.

Background:
Output is part of the API surface because agents and tests will consume it directly. Pretty output must remain stable and JSON schemas must remain predictable.

Key considerations:
- Right-aligned line numbers for read output.
- Context neighborhood merging and anchor marking.
- JSON must include file metadata needed by optimistic concurrency.
- Keep formatters separate from business logic to simplify testing.

Done means:
- read/index/verify/stats/dry-run/receipt formatters exist as applicable for the currently implemented features.
- Snapshot tests can lock the output shape.

title: Implement atomic writeback and permission preservation
description:
Implement writeback.rs atomic file replacement.

Background:
Atomic writeback is the safety boundary for every mutation. All modifying commands should funnel through this module so partial writes are impossible under normal failure modes.

Key considerations:
- Temp file must be created in the target directory.
- Flush + sync before persist.
- Preserve permissions where supported.
- Keep rendering edge cases correct for zero-byte results and trailing newline preservation.

Done means:
- atomic_write and permission preservation exist.
- Tests verify round-trip behavior, empty writes, same-directory temp creation, and permission preservation behavior.

title: Implement shared document mutation primitives for all write commands
description:
Purpose:
Design and implement the shared in-memory mutation primitives that all mutating commands will reuse.

Why this bead was added:
The first graph let edit/insert/delete land directly, and later patch/swap/move/indent depended on them implicitly. That is workable, but it pushes too much command-specific logic into each command and risks duplicated line-manipulation behavior. A dedicated mutation-engine bead makes the system more coherent for users because commands will behave more consistently and edge-case fixes will apply everywhere.

Likely scope:
- Single-line replacement
- Inclusive-range replacement collapsed to one line
- Insert-after-anchor semantics
- Delete-line semantics
- Line renumber/hash recomputation strategy after mutation
- Shared before/after change capture hooks that later support dry-run and receipts

Done means:
- Core mutation operations exist in reusable form independent of clap command glue.
- Commands become thin wrappers around shared semantics.

title: Ship foundational CLI commands
description:
Purpose:
Ship the minimum end-to-end command set that proves the linehash model works in practice: read, index, edit, insert, and delete.

Why:
This is the first point where the tool delivers direct agent value. It also exercises the full stack from load -> resolve -> mutate -> atomic rewrite -> render.

Success definition:
- A user can inspect a file, identify anchors, mutate by anchor, and trust that stale/ambiguous edits are rejected.

title: Implement read command with JSON and context views
description:
Implement the read command, including full-file pretty output, JSON output, and focused context mode.

Background:
read is the primary discovery command. If this command is awkward or unstable, every downstream workflow becomes harder.

Key considerations:
- Full-file output and context mode are distinct UX paths.
- Context neighborhoods around one or more anchors must merge cleanly.
- JSON must include mtime/inode data for later mutation guards.

Done means:
- read works in pretty and JSON modes.
- --anchor/--context behavior matches the plan.
- Integration + snapshot coverage exists.

title: Implement index command
description:
Implement the index command.

Background:
index is a lightweight companion to read. It should provide hashes-only output for automation or low-token inspection.

Done means:
- Pretty and JSON forms exist.
- Output shape is stable and tested.

title: Implement edit, insert, and delete commands
description:
Implement the first mutation commands: edit, insert, and delete.

Background:
These are the minimum operations needed to prove the linehash editing model. They also create the contract future mutation commands will follow.

Key considerations:
- Single-line content only in v1.
- Range edit collapses inclusive range to one new line.
- Preserve newline style and trailing newline.
- All writes route through atomic writeback.

Done means:
- edit, insert, and delete work in the intended cases and reject stale/ambiguous anchors correctly.
- Integration tests cover round-trip behavior and formatting preservation.

title: Add transaction safety and observability features
description:
Purpose:
Add transactionality and observability features that turn single-line editing into a safe batch-editing pipeline for agents.

Scope:
- Concurrency guards
n- verify / grep / annotate
- patch transaction engine
- dry-run previews
- receipts and audit log

Why:
These features reduce failure modes in realistic multi-step agent workflows and make edits inspectable, auditable, and concurrency-safe.

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: This epic captures the point where linehash stops being merely an edit primitive and becomes an agent-safe editing system. verify, patch, concurrency guards, and receipts together form the core reliability story.

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Second refinement: split dry-run from receipts/audit logging. Users benefit from preview safety earlier, and implementation can validate preview semantics independently from persistent audit behavior.

title: Implement mtime/inode concurrency guards
description:
Implement optimistic concurrency guard support.

Background:
The plan assumes agents may operate concurrently. mtime/inode guards prevent a mutation from applying to a file that changed after it was read.

Key considerations:
- Capture metadata at read/load time.
- Wire --expect-mtime and --expect-inode into all mutation paths.
- Fail before touching the file.

Done means:
- Shared helper(s) exist.
- Foundational mutation commands reject stale files with the expected error.

title: Implement verify command with aggregate resolution reporting
description:
Implement verify as a bulk pre-flight anchor checker.

Background:
verify exists to separate anchor validation from mutation. This is especially valuable in batch or agent workflows where we want a cheap pre-check.

Key considerations:
- Do not short-circuit after the first failure.
- Exit code semantics matter.
- Output should distinguish success, ambiguity, and failure clearly.

title: Implement grep command returning anchors for regex matches
description:
Implement grep as regex search returning anchor-formatted results.

Background:
grep bridges content discovery and anchor-based editing. It should make it easy to locate actionable lines without a full read.

Key considerations:
- Regex compilation errors should be explicit and actionable.
- JSON output should reuse the filtered line schema rather than inventing a new one.

title: Implement annotate command for content-to-anchor lookup
description:
Implement annotate for reverse lookup from content to anchor.

Background:
annotate supports workflows where an agent knows what a line says but not where it lives. It is the content->anchor inverse of read/index.

Key considerations:
- Substring mode first, regex optional via flag.
- --expect-one should turn ambiguity into an error.
- Output should be directly reusable as anchor input to mutation commands.

title: Implement patch transaction engine and command
description:
Implement patch.rs and the patch command for atomic multi-op transactions.

Background:
patch is one of the most important compound features in the entire plan. It converts a sequence of fragile, stateful edits into one snapshot-based transaction.

Key considerations:
- Deserialize and validate the patch file schema.
- Resolve all anchors against one document snapshot before mutating anything.
- Preserve declared op order in-memory.
- If one op fails, nothing is written.

Done means:
- patch can apply a valid multi-op file transaction.
- A stale/ambiguous/not-found op leaves the file byte-identical.
- dry-run and failure indexing semantics are in place or supported cleanly.

title: Implement dry-run previews, receipts, and audit logging
description:
Implement dry-run, receipts, and audit-log support across mutation commands.

Background:
The project is not only about safe mutation, but also about inspectability. dry-run enables preview, while receipts and JSONL audit logs create an observable edit history and future basis for undo.

Key considerations:
- Keep dry-run side-effect-free under all circumstances.
- Receipts must capture before/after line content and hashes plus whole-file hashes.
- Audit logging must append only for successful operations.

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Marked as superseded umbrella after refinement split into linehash-39q.5.7 (dry-run) and linehash-39q.5.8 (receipts/audit-log). Keep only as a historical grouping reference, not as the preferred implementation unit.

title: Implement dry-run preview pipeline for mutation commands
description:
Purpose:
Implement dry-run as a first-class preview path for mutation commands.

Why split this out:
Dry-run has different concerns from receipts/audit logging. It is about pre-write simulation, diff rendering, and proposed document inspection. Keeping it separate makes rollout easier and reduces the chance that observability plumbing delays a very high-value safety feature.

Done means:
- Mutation commands can preview changes without writing bytes.
- --dry-run --json can emit proposed post-mutation state where appropriate.

title: Implement receipts and persistent audit-log recording
description:
Purpose:
Implement receipts and audit-log append behavior.

Why split this out:
Receipts and audit logs are post-success observability concerns, distinct from preview simulation. Splitting them clarifies sequencing and lets dry-run become available earlier for users.

Done means:
- Receipt structures and rendering exist.
- Successful mutations can emit receipts and append JSONL audit records.
- Failed operations never append audit entries.

title: Implement second-wave power features
description:
Purpose:
Implement the second wave of high-leverage commands that remove common multi-step failure modes and improve large-file ergonomics.

Scope:
- swap / move / indent
- find-block
- stats
- from-diff
- merge-patches

Why:
These commands compound on the stable core and transaction system. They should be sequenced after the patch/verification machinery exists so they can reuse established patterns and tests.

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: These features are high-value but should not be allowed to distort the core. They are sequenced after the transaction layer so they can reuse stable patterns for resolution, mutation, dry-run, and observability.

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Second refinement: split swap from move. They are related, but move has materially trickier semantics; separating them improves test isolation and makes earlier user value available.

title: Implement swap and move commands
description:
Implement swap and move.

Background:
These commands eliminate a common agent anti-pattern: simulating reordering with multiple fragile edits. They should behave as single atomic transformations against one snapshot.

Key considerations:
- Same-line swap should error.
- Move semantics must be clear when source/target indices shift after removal.
- Reversibility tests are important because these are structural transforms.

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Marked as superseded umbrella after refinement split into linehash-39q.6.7 (swap) and linehash-39q.6.8 (move). Prefer the split tasks for actual implementation tracking.

title: Implement indent command with range-safe indent/dedent
description:
Implement indent range transforms.

Background:
Indentation changes are a classic source of accidental corruption, especially in Python/YAML. This command exists to make indentation explicit, anchored, and testable.

Key considerations:
- Operate over resolved inclusive ranges.
- Detect indentation mode and reject mixed indentation inside the target range.
- Dedent must detect underflow and name the offending line.

title: Implement find-block command and block boundary discovery
description:
Implement find-block and its supporting block-discovery logic.

Background:
This feature lets agents discover a safe range around a line without guessing block boundaries manually. It is powerful, but dangerous if wrong, so ambiguity must surface as errors.

Key considerations:
- Brace-based traversal for C-family files.
- Indent-based traversal for Python/YAML.
- Language hints from extension first, heuristics second.
- Never return a range that is not justifiable from the anchor line.

title: Implement stats command for collisions and token budgeting
description:
Implement stats for token budgeting and anchor quality reporting.

Background:
stats helps an agent decide whether to read a full file, use context mode, or qualify anchors more aggressively. It makes the collision-heavy nature of 2-char hashes explicit instead of surprising users later.

Key considerations:
- Count collisions accurately.
- Keep token estimates simple and stable.
- Advice output should explain why longer hashes may be desirable in future versions without changing v1 behavior.

title: Implement from-diff compiler from unified diff to patch JSON
description:
Implement unified diff import.

Background:
from-diff makes linehash interoperable with diff-producing tools and allows external diff logic to compile into a safe patch transaction.

Key considerations:
- Parse unified diffs carefully.
- Match hunks against current file content, not assumptions about an old snapshot.
- Fail loudly on mismatch rather than applying a near miss.

title: Implement merge-patches composition and conflict detection
description:
Implement merge-patches.

Background:
This feature composes independent patch files or surfaces conflicts explicitly. It is effectively the patch-level analogue of safe merge logic.

Key considerations:
- Resolve both patches against the same current base file.
- Report all conflicts, not just the first.
- Include non-conflicting ops in output even when conflicts exist, so human follow-up is informed.

title: Implement swap command
description:
Purpose:
Implement swap as an atomic line transposition command.

Why split this out:
swap is smaller and conceptually cleaner than move. It is a better first structural-mutation command and provides earlier value/testing confidence.

Done means:
- Two distinct anchors can be swapped atomically.
- Same-line swap errors cleanly.
- Round-trip reversibility is tested.

title: Implement move command
description:
Purpose:
Implement move as an atomic relocation command.

Why split this out:
move has trickier index-shift semantics than swap. Splitting it keeps the logic and tests more focused and makes regressions easier to isolate.

Done means:
- Source line can be moved after a target anchor in a single snapshot-based operation.
- Index-shift edge cases are covered in tests.

title: Implement watch and explode/implode workflows
description:
Purpose:
Implement watch, explode, and implode as integration-oriented tools that extend linehash beyond single-command editing into monitoring and filesystem-native workflows.

Why:
These are useful, but they are downstream from the core editing model and should not destabilize it. They therefore sit later in the dependency graph.

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Second refinement: split explode from implode. Export shape and safe reassembly are different concerns and should not be hidden inside one oversized implementation bead.

title: Implement watch command for live hash recomputation
description:
Implement watch using notify.

Background:
watch turns linehash from a one-shot inspector into a change observer that can recompute anchors after file saves. In v1, correctness and clean exit semantics matter more than fancy daemon behavior.

Key considerations:
- --once default semantics.
- JSON event streaming for agent consumption.
- Avoid platform-specific surprises where possible; fail clearly when unsupported.

title: Implement explode and implode round-trip workflow
description:
Implement explode and implode.

Background:
These commands externalize the linehash model into the filesystem itself: each line becomes its own file and the filename encodes the anchor. This enables diff/status workflows that are naturally line-scoped.

Key considerations:
- Metadata file must preserve newline/trailing-newline/source-path data needed for round-trip.
- implode must reject dirty directories rather than guessing which files matter.
- Round-trip byte identity is the core acceptance property.

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Marked as superseded umbrella after refinement split into linehash-39q.7.3 (explode) and linehash-39q.7.4 (implode). Prefer the split tasks for execution and validation.

title: Implement explode command
description:
Purpose:
Implement explode as one-way decomposition of a file into line-addressed files plus metadata.

Why split this out:
explode and implode are related but not identical risks. explode is primarily about faithful export shape; implode is about validation and reassembly safety. Splitting them makes the workflow clearer and improves testing focus.

Done means:
- explode writes the expected per-line files and metadata format.
- Existing-dir and force semantics are tested.

title: Implement implode command
description:
Purpose:
Implement implode as validated reassembly from an exploded directory back into a source file.

Why split this out:
implode is where the major safety checks live: dirty directory rejection, metadata validation, ordering, and atomic writeback. It deserves its own bead rather than being hidden inside a combined workflow issue.

Done means:
- implode validates structure and metadata.
- Dirty directories are rejected clearly.
- Explode->implode round-trip can be proven byte-identical.

title: Harden, validate, and prepare v1 release
description:
Purpose:
Close the gap between 'works in happy paths' and 'ready to ship'. This includes error polish, fixture coverage, acceptance validation, README verification, and release gates.

Why:
The plan's value proposition depends on trust. Trust comes from consistent failure modes, strong test coverage, and repeatable release checks.

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: This epic exists because the stated product promise is trustworthiness, not just functionality. A feature-complete but weakly validated CLI would miss the point of the project.

comments.author: quangdang
comments.text: Revision note: separated release quality gates from full-scope acceptance validation. This keeps the graph honest: quality checks prove code health, while acceptance validation proves the full user-facing promise.

title: Run release quality gates and verify shipped behavior
description:
Create and complete the quality gate issue for release readiness.

Scope:
- cargo fmt --check
- cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings
- cargo test
- cargo install smoke test
- verify README examples and CLI contract consistency

Why:
This is the final proof that the implementation is coherent as a shipped CLI rather than merely passing isolated unit tests.

title: Polish edge cases, binary detection, and recovery hints
description:
Audit and polish all user-facing errors and edge-case handling.

Background:
The plan promises recovery-oriented errors and explicit refusal to guess. This issue exists to ensure those guarantees remain true after all features land.

Scope:
- Error text consistency
- Recovery hints
- blank-line behavior
- binary-file rejection polish
- snapshot coverage for major error forms

title: Validate all PLAN.md acceptance criteria end-to-end
description:
Map the implementation against the explicit v1 acceptance criteria from PLAN.md and close any remaining gaps.

Why:
The acceptance list is effectively the product definition. This issue ensures the team validates against that list directly rather than relying on an implicit sense of completeness.

Done means:
- Each acceptance criterion has corresponding evidence: tests, command behavior, or documented rationale.
- Any unmet criterion is captured as a follow-up issue rather than silently ignored.

title: Add and validate hash benchmark target
description:
Add the hash benchmark and verify the expected performance envelope.

Background:
The plan includes a benchmark target because linehash should feel cheap enough to use frequently in agent loops.

Key considerations:
- Benchmark the hashing path in isolation.
- Avoid conflating I/O and hashing costs.
- Record results in a way future sessions can compare.

title: Validate README and Claude Code integration protocol as shipped UX
description:
Purpose:
Prepare the README and Claude Code integration guidance as a tested product surface, not an afterthought.

Why this bead was added:
A large part of linehash's value is how it changes editing workflows for agents. That value only materializes if the docs and integration protocol are explicit, validated, and aligned with the shipped CLI.

Scope:
- README command examples
- CLAUDE.md integration snippet verification
- Guidance on when to use stats vs read vs context mode
- Guidance on stale/ambiguous/concurrency recovery loops

Done means:
- Documentation examples are verified against the implementation.
- The integration protocol is trustworthy enough to copy into downstream projects.
